title: Number of ZigZag Arrays I
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: C folder with the minimum-memory champion. The recommended and fastest-runtime proposal is implemented in C++; see crossReferences.

variants.languageReason: The memory objective is a scalar modular recurrence over at most 2000 ranks. C uses one raw stack buffer and scalar counters with no container, object, class, heap, or managed-runtime overhead. The in-place reversed-prefix transform halves the explicit DP buffer count relative to the C++ runtime proposal.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Best for the recommended/runtime sibling because two buffers and pointer swaps give the cleanest fast path, but it intentionally spends a second DP buffer.
Selected. A single int[2000] stack buffer plus scalar counters is the smallest practical auxiliary layout for this recurrence.
Native and memory-safe, but checked indexing and wrapper overhead do not reduce the one-buffer state below the C implementation.
Slices can represent the DP, but runtime and slice metadata are heavier than one raw C stack buffer.
Managed runtimes and primitive-array object headers are not competitive for the least-memory axis.
Dynamic numeric arrays and VM/runtime baselines are far larger than a native 2000-int stack buffer.

variants.approachJustification: Use the same mirrored one-direction DP as the C++ proposal, but update the reversed-prefix transform in-place. While scanning y upward, old high mirrored values are consumed first; old low values that will be needed later are saved into those consumed high slots.

reasoningSummary: The comparison signs must alternate, so a rank DP by final value and last sign is sufficient. Downward counts mirror upward counts by value reversal. The C memory proposal computes the mirrored recurrence in-place to use one DP buffer.
timeComplexity: O(n * m), where m = r - l + 1
